summary: Delivers an enhanced academic transcript that equips every student to turn the knowledge and capabilities developed through their course and broader King’s experience into a credible story of what they can contribute.
detailSummary:
Every student will have an enhanced academic transcript that grows with them through their time at King’s. In one digital view, it will bring together academic achievement, a visual map of their skills, recognised experiences, institutional microcredentials and evidence contributed by the student. They will be able to see where their capabilities came from, understand whether they are certified, evidenced or claimed, and draw on the record to tell credible stories about what they can contribute.

Building on or potentially succeeding the Higher Education Achievement Record, the transcript will support student articulation while giving King’s a stronger account of its educational value. Students will be able to select relevant evidence for applications, profiles, portfolios and interviews. Responsibly aggregated profiles will help programme teams, faculties and King’s demonstrate the knowledge and capabilities developed through courses and the broader experience of learning and development.

This deliverable will define the vision, uses, information model and requirements for the enhanced transcript. The digital capability itself will be delivered through the Single Student App / Digital Portal project within the Education Framework’s digital strand. King’s Edge will specify what that platform must enable, including skills visualisation and tracking, capturing and organising evidence, credential issuing and verification, transcript generation and appropriately governed aggregate insight.

caseForChange.problem:
The academic transcript provides an authoritative record of modules, marks and classification, but the knowledge and capabilities developed through a course remain largely buried within those outcomes. Learning arising through students’ broader experience of learning and development can be occluded from the record altogether.

Students are therefore left to identify, evidence and translate much of the value of their education for themselves. Those with greater confidence, networks or familiarity with professional language are better placed to build a compelling account of what they have learned and can contribute.

King’s does not yet have a sufficiently rich, connected view of the capabilities developed across its students. This limits its ability to demonstrate the collective relevance and value of its education to the wider world.
caseForChange.opportunity:
King’s can transform the academic transcript into a richer, evolving account of each student’s learning. Alongside academic outcomes, it can make visible the knowledge and capabilities developed through their course, together with relevant learning and development across their broader King’s experience. A skills profile, recognised experiences, institutional microcredentials and student-contributed evidence can feed the transcript, with clear distinctions between what King’s has certified, what the student has evidenced and what they have claimed.

Students can draw on this record to communicate a distinctive and credible account of what they have learned and can contribute to different audiences. Access during study can also help them reflect on their progress and consider what they want to develop next, connecting the transcript to King’s wider work on student purpose.

Responsibly aggregated insight from these records can give King’s a stronger view of the capabilities its education develops and a richer basis for demonstrating its relevance to the wider world.
caseForChange.whyNow:
For much of the recent past, a degree has operated as a passport. Its title and classification were widely treated as sufficient shorthand for a graduate’s capabilities and future potential. That assumption is weakening. Confidence in the graduate premium is under pressure as graduate job security and access to well-paid work become less certain, while government, business and industry increasingly question the vocational relevance of university education.

King’s can no longer rely on the degree label to carry the full weight of its educational value. The disciplinary knowledge, higher-order reasoning, judgement and broader capabilities developed through university remain vital and become more important in a world shaped by AI. These gains must be brought to the surface so that students can credibly articulate what their education has enabled them to know, do and contribute, and King’s can respond assertively to external scepticism.

Connected work on a shared skills language, student purpose, recognised learning and institutional microcredentials now provides the means to describe those gains more fully. The Single Student App / Digital Portal creates a live delivery window. Defining the enhanced transcript now will ensure these elements converge into a coherent and trusted account of learning rather than remain disconnected.
